# Value Chain & Conversion Guide: Petrochemical Yields & Cost Propagation

This guide compiles standard petrochemical consumption factors, yields, and mathematical cost-propagation formulas for the **Crude $\rightarrow$ Naphtha $\rightarrow$ Benzene $\rightarrow$ Aniline $\rightarrow$ MDI** chain. 

It is structured to enable the RAG system to reason over feedstock cost movements and calculate their downstream variable-cost impacts.

---

## 1. Materials Balance & Consumption Factors

In industrial chemical manufacturing, the mass relationship between raw materials and intermediates is governed by stoichiometry and plant-specific yield efficiencies:

$$\text{Consumption Factor (ton/ton)} = \frac{\text{Mass of Feedstock Consumed (tons)}}{\text{Mass of Product Produced (tons)}}$$

### 1.1 Upstream: Crude Oil to Benzene
Benzene is not produced directly from crude oil; it is extracted from refinery co-product streams:
*   **Crude to Naphtha Yield:** Typical atmospheric distillation yields **15–20 wt%** straight-run naphtha from light sweet crude.
*   **Naphtha to Benzene (Catalytic Reforming):** Reforming naphtha yields a BTX (Benzene, Toluene, Xylene) reformate stream. The net benzene yield from naphtha is typically **5–12 wt%** depending on reforming severity and reformer design (e.g., Continuous Catalyst Regeneration).
*   **Pygas Steam Cracking:** Cracking naphtha to ethylene yields pyrolysis gasoline (Pygas), which contains **30–40 wt%** benzene.

### 1.2 Intermediate: Benzene to Aniline
The chemical pathway is:
$$\text{Benzene (C}_6\text{H}_6) \xrightarrow{+\text{HNO}_3} \text{Nitrobenzene (C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NO}_2) \xrightarrow{+3\text{H}_2} \text{Aniline (C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NH}_2)$$

*   **Molar Mass Transformation:**
    *   Benzene: $78.11 \text{ g/mol}$
    *   Aniline: $93.13 \text{ g/mol}$
*   **Stoichiometric Ratio:** $1 \text{ mol Benzene} \rightarrow 1 \text{ mol Aniline}$ (theoretical consumption factor: $78.11 / 93.13 = 0.838 \text{ ton/ton}$).
*   **Practical Consumption Factor:** Due to process inefficiencies, side reactions, and waste recovery loops:
    *   **Standard Factor:** **0.88 – 0.92 tons of Benzene per ton of Aniline** (typically modeled at **0.90** for standard plants).

### 1.3 Downstream: Aniline to MDI
The chemical pathway is:
$$\text{2 Aniline (C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NH}_2) + \text{Formaldehyde (CH}_2\text{O}) \rightarrow \text{MDA (C}_{13}\text{H}_{14}\text{N}_2) \xrightarrow{+2\text{COCl}_2} \text{MDI (C}_{15}\text{H}_{10}\text{N}_2\text{O}_2)$$

*   **Molar Mass Transformation:**
    *   Aniline: $93.13 \text{ g/mol}$
    *   Formaldehyde: $30.03 \text{ g/mol}$
    *   MDI: $250.25 \text{ g/mol}$
*   **Stoichiometric Ratio:** $2 \text{ moles Aniline} \rightarrow 1 \text{ mole MDI}$ (theoretical consumption factor: $2 \times 93.13 / 250.25 = 0.744 \text{ ton/ton}$).
*   **Practical Consumption Factor:** Due to oligomerization ratios (polymeric MDI vs. pure MDI split) and condensation losses:
    *   **Standard Factor:** **0.74 – 0.78 tons of Aniline per ton of MDI** (typically modeled at **0.76**).
    *   **Formaldehyde Factor:** **0.09 – 0.11 tons of Formaldehyde per ton of MDI** (typically modeled at **0.10**).

---

## 2. Cost Propagation Sensitivity Formulas

---

## 2. Cost Propagation Sensitivity Formulas

To calculate how an upstream price shift in Crude or Benzene affects downstream MDI margins, we use linear cost-propagation formulas based on the consumption factors:

### 2.1 Benzene to Aniline Variable Cost Formula
Let $C_{\text{Aniline}}$ be the variable cost of Aniline ($/ton), $P_{\text{Benzene}}$ be the market price of Benzene ($/ton), and $U_{\text{Aniline}}$ be the utilities/fixed-conversion cost of the aniline plant:

$$C_{\text{Aniline}} = (0.90 \times P_{\text{Benzene}}) + U_{\text{Aniline}}$$

*   *Sensitivity:* A **$100/ton** increase in Benzene prices increases the production cost of Aniline by **$90/ton**.

*   *Sensitivity:* A **$100/ton** increase in Benzene prices increases the production cost of Aniline by **$90/ton**.

### 2.2 Aniline to MDI Variable Cost Formula
Let $C_{\text{MDI}}$ be the variable production cost of MDI ($/ton), $P_{\text{Aniline}}$ be the price of Aniline ($/ton), $P_{\text{Form}}$ be the price of Formaldehyde ($/ton), and $U_{\text{MDI}}$ be the processing utilities/phosgenation cost:

$$C_{\text{MDI}} = (0.76 \times P_{\text{Aniline}}) + (0.10 \times P_{\text{Form}}) + U_{\text{MDI}}$$

*   *Sensitivity:* A **$100/ton** increase in Aniline prices increases the variable cost of MDI by **$76/ton**.

*   *Sensitivity:* A **$100/ton** increase in Aniline prices increases the variable cost of MDI by **$76/ton**.

### 2.3 End-to-End Chain Sensitivity Equation
By substituting the Aniline formula into the MDI formula, we get the end-to-end sensitivity of MDI cost to changes in Benzene prices:

$$\Delta C_{\text{MDI}} = 0.76 \times (0.90 \times \Delta P_{\text{Benzene}}) = 0.684 \times \Delta P_{\text{Benzene}}$$

*   *Rule of Thumb:* **Every $1.00 move in Benzene prices propagates to a $0.68 move in MDI variable production costs.**
*   *Lag Factor:* Because feedstock contracts are typically settled on a monthly basis, this cost change takes **14 to 28 days** to be fully realized on the MDI manufacturer's balance sheet.

---

## 3. Crude Oil to Benzene Price Correlation Index

---

## 3. Crude Oil to Benzene Price Correlation Index

While Benzene is not converted directly from Crude, their historical spot prices are highly correlated due to Naphtha linkages.

$$\text{Benzene Price (\$/ton)} \approx \left(\text{Crude Price (\$/bbl)} \times 7.3 \times 1.15 \right) + \text{Refining Spread}$$

Where:
*   $7.3$ is the average barrels of naphtha per metric ton.
*   $1.15$ is the aromatic premium reformer conversion factor.
*   *Reformer Spread:* Typically ranges between **$150/ton and $250/ton** depending on global refining margins and aromatics demand.

# Data Analyst Interview Study Guide

> [!TIP]
> Use this guide as a quick refresher before your interview. Focus on understanding the *concepts* and *when to use them* rather than memorizing every formula. Interviewers often care more about your problem-solving approach.

## 1. Basic Statistical Analysis

### Core Concepts
* **Descriptive Statistics:** Summarizes and describes features of a dataset.
    * **Measures of Central Tendency:** Mean (average), Median (middle value), Mode (most frequent value). *Interview tip: Know when to use median over mean (e.g., when data has extreme outliers, like income).*
    * **Measures of Spread/Dispersion:** Variance, Standard Deviation (average distance from the mean), Range, Interquartile Range (IQR).
* **Inferential Statistics:** Drawing conclusions about a population based on a sample.
    * **Hypothesis Testing:** Formulating a Null Hypothesis ($H_0$, no effect) and an Alternative Hypothesis ($H_A$, there is an effect).

* **P-value:** The probability of observing your results if the null hypothesis is true. A p-value < 0.05 is typically considered statistically significant (you reject the null hypothesis).
    * **Confidence Intervals:** A range of values that likely contains the true population parameter, with a specified level of confidence (e.g., 95%).

### Key Topics for Interviews
* **Correlation vs. Causation:** Correlation measures the strength of the linear relationship between two variables (-1 to 1). Remember the golden rule: *Correlation does not imply causation*.
* **Normal Distribution (Bell Curve) & Central Limit Theorem (CLT):** The CLT states that the distribution of sample means approximates a normal distribution as the sample size gets larger, regardless of the population's distribution.
* **A/B Testing:** A common application of hypothesis testing in business (e.g., comparing two versions of a webpage to see which generates more clicks).
* **Linear vs. Logistic Regression:**
    * *Linear:* Predicts a continuous value (e.g., predicting house prices). Uses R-squared to measure goodness of fit.

* *Linear:* Predicts a continuous value (e.g., predicting house prices). Uses R-squared to measure goodness of fit.
    * *Logistic:* Predicts a categorical outcome, usually binary (e.g., will a customer churn? Yes/No).

---

## 2. Excel (Focus on Pivot Tables)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Interviewers testing Excel skills usually want to ensure you know how to clean data, summarize it dynamically, and perform lookups.

### Pivot Tables Basics
A Pivot Table is a powerful tool to calculate, summarize, and analyze data, allowing you to see comparisons, patterns, and trends.
* **Rows & Columns:** Grouping your data (e.g., Rows = Region, Columns = Year).
* **Values:** The quantitative data you are aggregating (e.g., Sum of Sales, Count of Customers, Average of Profit).
* **Filters:** Restricting the data shown in the entire Pivot Table.

### Common Interview Questions & Best Practices
* **Data Preparation:** Always ensure your source data has headers, no blank rows/columns, and consistent data types before creating a Pivot Table.
* **Using Tables as Source:** Always convert your source data to an Excel Table (`Ctrl + T`). This ensures that if new rows are added to the source, the Pivot Table will include them upon refresh.
* **Calculated Fields vs. Calculated Items:**
    * *Calculated Field:* A new field created by performing calculations on existing fields (e.g., `Profit = Revenue - Cost`).
    * *Calculated Item:* A calculation performed on specific items within a single field.
* **Slicers & Timelines:** Visual buttons used to filter Pivot Tables quickly, essential for creating interactive dashboards in Excel.
* **Refreshing:** Remember that Pivot Tables do not update automatically when source data changes. You must right-click and select "Refresh" or set it to refresh upon opening the file.

### Other Essential Excel Functions to Know
* `XLOOKUP` (or `VLOOKUP` / `INDEX+MATCH`) for joining data.
* `SUMIFS` / `COUNTIFS` for conditional aggregations.
* `IFERROR` for handling `#N/A` or `#DIV/0!` errors cleanly.

---

## 3. Working with Large Data Sets (Power BI & Business Analytics)

### Power BI Core Workflow
1. **Get Data:** Connecting to data sources (SQL, Excel, APIs).
2. **Power Query (Transform Data):** The ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) engine. *Key interview topic.* Be prepared to discuss steps like pivoting/unpivoting columns, removing nulls, splitting columns, and appending/merging queries.
3. **Data Modeling:** Building relationships between tables.
    * **Star Schema:** The ideal model structure in Power BI. Consists of a central **Fact Table** (quantitative data like Sales) surrounded by **Dimension Tables** (descriptive data like Dates, Products, Customers).
    * Relationships are usually 1-to-many (1-to-*).
4. **DAX (Data Analysis Expressions):** The formula language of Power BI.

4. **DAX (Data Analysis Expressions):** The formula language of Power BI.
    * **Calculated Columns:** Evaluated row-by-row during data load. Takes up memory. Use sparingly.
    * **Measures:** Evaluated on-the-fly based on report filters/context. Takes up CPU. Use for aggregations (e.g., `Total Sales = SUM(Sales[Amount])`).
    * Important DAX functions: `CALCULATE` (changes filter context), `SUMX` (iterator function), `RELATED`.
5. **Visualizations & Dashboards:** Choosing the right chart and publishing to the Power BI Service.

### Handling Large Datasets (Performance Tuning)
> [!TIP]
> If asked how to handle massive datasets (e.g., millions/billions of rows), mention these optimization techniques.

* **Import vs. DirectQuery Mode:**
    * *Import Mode:* Loads data directly into Power BI's memory cache (fast, but memory-limited).
    * *DirectQuery:* Queries the underlying database in real-time (slower, but essential if data is too large to fit in memory or requires real-time updates).
* **Query Folding in Power Query:** Pushing data transformations back to the source database (like SQL Server) rather than letting Power Query's engine do the heavy lifting locally.
* **Removing Unnecessary Data:** Delete columns and filter out rows you don't need *before* loading the data into the model to reduce file size.
* **Incremental Refresh:** Instead of reloading the entire historical dataset every time, only load new or updated data.
* **Aggregations:** Creating summary tables in the database (e.g., daily sales instead of transaction-level sales) to reduce the amount of data Power BI needs to process for high-level dashboards.

### The Analyst's Probable Monday Morning Reality

Understanding this workflow is the entire pitch for ChemSignals. Before any pivot table or PowerBI dashboard is refreshed, an analyst is doing this by hand:

1. **Data pull (30–45 min)** — Opens Excel. Manually copies commodity prices from a Bloomberg terminal, CME Group website, or Reuters feed into a raw data tab. Pulls FRED macro data separately — either by downloading CSVs from fred.stlouisfed.org or running a legacy VBA macro against the FRED API. May also check chemical trade publications (ICIS, Chemical Week) for spot price updates.

2. **Data cleaning (20–30 min)** — Strips weekends and holidays. Normalizes units (barrels vs. tonnes, $/bbl vs. $/t). Reconciles any missing values or source conflicts. Ensures consistent column headers so pivot tables don't break.

3. **Pivot table refresh (10 min)** — Triggers manual refresh. Rebuilds slicers if new categories were added. Exports a snapshot tab for the weekly email.

4. **PowerBI refresh (5–10 min)** — The PowerBI report is connected to the SharePoint-hosted Excel file. Triggers a manual data refresh in the Power BI Service. Checks that all visuals updated correctly.

5. **Commentary (30–45 min)** — The hardest part. Writes the "so what" paragraph by hand: a few bullet points in a Word doc or PowerPoint slide deck summarizing what moved and why it matters for margins. This is entirely manual and depends on the analyst's domain knowledge.

6. **Distribution** — Emails the PDF or a PowerBI link to leadership before the 9am meeting.

**Total time: 90–120 minutes every Monday morning, before any actual analysis begins.**

**Total time: 90–120 minutes every Monday morning, before any actual analysis begins.**

**The ChemSignals pitch:** Steps 1–4 are fully automated by `monday_brief.py` and the JSON bridge to the terminal. Step 5 is handled by Claude synthesis. The analyst's Monday morning is reduced to opening a fresh brief and the "Export Excel" button delivers a pivot-ready workbook directly into their existing PowerBI workflow — no disruption, no new tool adoption required.

**The broader enterprise reality:** Large organizations like Huntsman run multiple internal systems that do not communicate — SAP for financials and production, Ariba or similar for procurement, a separate commodity trading platform, quality management systems, logistics tools. Excel is the universal interchange format *because* it's the only thing every system can export to. Analysts spend significant time manually exporting from each system, reconciling the files, and rebuilding the same pivot tables. The inverse of the ChemSignals export — uploading any of those system exports and receiving a synthesized cross-system report against live market data — is the natural extension of this architecture and directly addresses a structural data problem every large chemical company has.

### A Note on SQL
For almost all roles involving large datasets, **SQL** is the fundamental language used to extract data *before* it gets to a tool like Power BI. Ensure you are fresh on `JOIN` types (Inner, Left), `GROUP BY`, `HAVING`, and potentially Window Functions (`ROW_NUMBER`, `RANK`).

## 4. The Huntsman Business Model & Data Strategy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> To stand out, frame your data analytics skills within the context of Huntsman's specific business model and value chain.

### Huntsman's Core Business Model
* **From Commodity to Specialty:** Huntsman transitioned from a bulk commodity chemical company (competing on volume) to a **pure-play specialty chemical company**. They buy intermediate building blocks and convert them into highly engineered, high-margin formulated systems.
* **Key Divisions:**
    1. **Polyurethanes:** The "crown jewel" (global leader in MDI for insulation, auto seating, and footwear).
    2. **Performance Products:** Amines and maleic anhydride for fuels, lubricants, and daily chemicals.
    3. **Textile Effects:** Advanced dyes and treatments (water-resistance, flame-retardancy).
    4. **Advanced Materials:** High-performance epoxy and polyurethane for aerospace and automotive lightweighting.

### Applying Your Background to Huntsman
As a Data Scientist with a business mind, emphasize how your past projects map to their value chain:
* **The Aramco Context (Upstream):** Huntsman buys basic feedstocks (like those Aramco produces). Your understanding of Aramco’s end-to-end integration helps you analyze how global feedstock pricing and energy trends squeeze or expand a specialty chemist's margins.
* **The DSM Context (The Transition):** Like DSM, Huntsman shifted from selling by the kiloton to **selling by value-in-use**. You understand the analytics needed to support high-margin, innovation-driven specialty chemicals.

* **The AkzoNobel Context (Downstream):** AkzoNobel is a major downstream customer (resins/coatings), mirroring the exact markets Huntsman's Advanced Materials division supplies. You understand the downstream demand signals.

### Your Data Edge: Signals & Trust
* **Macro Signals & Real-Time Analytics:** Highlight your experience building real-time macro signal platforms (using APIs from LME/CME) to provide executives with actionable, high-fidelity intelligence for strategic bets.
* **Trust & AI Safety:** Emphasize your philosophy: *"If you can't trust it, you can't depend on it."* Discuss your work using agents to detect anomalies in real-time external data streams, generating reliable health indicators for the business.

## 5. Chemical Industry Vertical Terminology (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> Use these terms naturally in your answers to show you understand the structural economics of the industry.

* **Upstream:** Refers to the origin point of the value chain. In chemicals, this means the extraction and production of basic raw materials like crude oil and natural gas (e.g., Saudi Aramco).
* **Midstream:** The processing, transportation, and production of bulk intermediate materials (e.g., turning crude into basic polymers). Companies like Dow and DuPont operate heavily here.
* **Downstream:** The manufacturing of finished, highly engineered products that are closer to the end consumer (e.g., AkzoNobel buying resins to make paints and coatings). 
* **Feedstock:** The raw materials required to fuel an industrial process. For Huntsman, changes in upstream energy prices directly affect the cost of their *feedstock*.

* **Commodity Chemicals:** Basic, undifferentiated chemicals produced in massive volumes. They compete almost entirely on scale and price (a "race to the bottom"). 
* **Specialty Chemicals:** High-value, highly formulated chemical blends designed for specific applications (like Huntsman's Polyurethanes). They compete on innovation and performance.
* **Value-in-Use:** A pricing and sales strategy used for specialty chemicals. Instead of pricing based on the cost to manufacture, it's priced based on the economic value it creates for the customer.
* **Margin Compression/Expansion:** The tightening (compression) or widening (expansion) of profit margins. If upstream feedstock costs rise but Huntsman can't pass those costs to downstream customers, their margins *compress*.

# Chemical Vertical Market Intelligence: MDI Value Chain & Competitor Analysis

This reference document compiles key industry intelligence for **ChemSignals (Avatar 1)**, detailing the petrochemical feedstock value chain and the profiles of major global producers.

---

## 1. The MDI Feedstock Value Chain

**MDI (Methylene Diphenyl Diisocyanate)** is the foundational chemical precursor for all polyurethane products (rigid insulation foam, flexible seating foam, coatings, and adhesives). 

The production of MDI follows a highly integrated chemical pathway starting from crude oil derivatives and moving through aniline:

```mermaid
graph TD
    Crude[Crude Oil / Naphtha] -->|Reforming| Benzene[Benzene]
    Benzene -->|Nitration| Nitrobenzene[Nitrobenzene]
    Nitrobenzene -->|Hydrogenation| Aniline[Aniline]
    Aniline -->|Formaldehyde Condensation| MDA[MDA: Methylenedianiline]
    MDA -->|Phosgenation| MDI[MDI: Methylene Diphenyl Diisocyanate]
    MDI -->|Formulation| Polyurethanes[Polyurethanes / Insulation & Auto]
```

### Steps in the Chain:
1. **Benzene to Nitrobenzene:** Benzene (an aromatic hydrocarbon derived from naphtha reforming or steam cracking) is reacted with nitric acid to produce nitrobenzene.
2. **Nitrobenzene to Aniline:** Nitrobenzene undergoes catalytic hydrogenation to yield **aniline**. Aniline is the single most critical intermediate in the chain, with **over 85% of global aniline** dedicated to MDI production.
3. **Aniline to MDA (Methylenedianiline):** Aniline is condensed with formaldehyde in the presence of hydrochloric acid.
4. **MDA to MDI:** MDA is reacted with phosgene to yield a mixture of MDI isomers (primarily 4,4'-MDI, 2,4'-MDI) and polymeric MDI (PMDI).

### Economic & Margin Dynamics:
* **Feedstock Transmission Lag:** Changes in WTI/Brent crude prices filter down to Benzene spot prices within days. However, because of contract pricing structures, it takes **2 to 4 weeks** for those feedstock cost movements to propagate through aniline to finished MDI costs.
* **Feedstock Exposure:** For MDI producers, benzene and aniline represent the largest variable cost drivers. Plant energy costs are also highly sensitive to regional natural gas prices (used for steam and heat generation).

---

## 2. Competitor Profiles & Capacity Analysis

The global MDI market is consolidated among a small group of large-scale chemical producers:

| Competitor | Est. Global MDI Capacity (per year) | Key Production Sites | Strategic Focus |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wanhua Chemical** | ~3.8M - 4.5M Metric Tons (World Leader) | Yantai, Ningbo, Fujian (China); Kazincbarcika (Hungary - BorsodChem) | Lowest cash-cost producer due to extreme scale and vertical integration. |
| **Covestro** | ~1.5M - 2.0M Metric Tons | Brunsbüttel, Krefeld-Uerdingen (Germany); Antwerp (Belgium); Shanghai (China); Baytown (Texas) | Transitioning to climate-neutral (mass-balance) MDI and high-margin specialty urethanes. |
| **BASF** | ~1.3M - 1.8M Metric Tons | Geismar (Louisiana); Chongqing, Shanghai (China); Antwerp (Belgium); Ludwigshafen (Germany) | "Verbund" integration, smart debottlenecking, and expanding Geismar capacity. |
| **Huntsman** | ~1.0M - 1.2M Metric Tons | Geismar (Louisiana); Rotterdam (Netherlands); Caojing (China) | Downstream integration into custom polyurethane systems (value-in-use pricing). |

### 2.1 Wanhua Chemical
Wanhua is the undisputed global volume leader, holding a **~60% market share in China** and roughly one-third of global capacity. 
* **Ningbo Site:** Expanded to 1.5M tons/year, making it one of the largest single MDI complexes in the world.
* **Fujian Site:** Upgraded in 2024 to 800kt/year, with ongoing expansions targeting 1.5M tons/year by mid-2026.
* **Cost Advantage:** Wanhua's proprietary technology and complete vertical integration give it the lowest production cash cost in the industry.

### 2.2 Covestro
Covestro operates under two segments: **Performance Materials** (commodity raw materials, bulk MDI, polyols) and **Solutions & Specialties** (custom formulations, coatings, elastomers).
* **Sustainability Leader:** Developed the *Desmodur® CQ* portfolio, utilizing plant-waste-based raw materials via mass-balancing to produce climate-neutral MDI.
* **Regional Footprint:** Major European presence (Antwerp, Germany) and North American presence (Baytown, Texas).

### 2.3 BASF
BASF manages MDI under its **Monomers division** (Materials segment).
* **Geismar Expansion:** Increasing its Geismar, Louisiana capacity from 380kt/year to **600kt/year** (final phase scheduled for Q3 2026 startup) to secure market share in North America.
* **Verbund Concept:** BASF relies on highly integrated sites where the output of one process serves as the input for another, minimizing logistics costs and energy use.
* **Efficiency Focus:** The 2026–2029 capital expenditure cycle focuses on maximizing utilization of existing capacities rather than funding new greenfield sites.

---

## 3. Margin & Pricing Mechanics

Specialty chemicals manufacturers operate on **value-in-use pricing** downstream, but are squeezed by **index-linked pricing** upstream.

* **Upstream Surcharges:** When raw material costs (Benzene) exceed defined thresholds, contract terms allow producers to pass through costs via surcharges.
* **Downstream Lag Risk:** If a producer misses an upstream price spike, they absorb 100% of the feedstock cost increase during the 2–4 week lag before surcharges can be legally activated under customer contracts.

# Chemical Industry Glossary: Terminology Reference Guide

This reference glossary provides definitions and terms of reference for feedstocks, processing units, logistics, and economic metrics in the petrochemical and polyurethane sectors. 

It is indexed in the RAG database to clarify industry jargon and standard concepts for natural language queries.

---

## 1. Feedstocks & Chemical Intermediates

*   **Naphtha:** A volatile, flammable liquid hydrocarbon mixture derived from crude oil refining. It is the primary liquid feedstock for steam crackers (to make olefins) and catalytic reformers (to make BTX aromatics).
*   **Benzene ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_6$):** A clear, colorless, highly flammable aromatic hydrocarbon. It is the primary aromatic feedstock used to produce nitrobenzene, aniline, and eventually MDI.
*   **Nitrobenzene ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NO}_2$):** An organic compound produced by the liquid-phase nitration of benzene with a mixture of nitric and sulfuric acids. It is used almost exclusively as a precursor to aniline.

*   **Aniline ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NH}_2$):** An organic base produced by the catalytic hydrogenation of nitrobenzene. It is the foundational building block for MDA and MDI.
*   **Formaldehyde ($\text{CH}_2\text{O}$):** A colorless, strong-smelling gas. It is reacted with aniline in the condensation step to produce MDA.
*   **MDA (Methylenedianiline):** An aromatic diamine intermediate produced by the acid-catalyzed condensation of aniline and formaldehyde. It is the direct precursor to MDI.
*   **Phosgene ($\text{COCl}_2$):** A highly toxic, colorless gas. It is reacted with MDA in the phosgenation step to introduce isocyanate groups, yielding MDI and hydrochloric acid as a co-product.

*   **MDI (Methylene Diphenyl Diisocyanate):** The primary diisocyanate monomer used in polyurethane manufacturing.
    *   **Pure MDI (Monomeric MDI):** Isomer mixture containing $>99\%$ diisocyanate (primarily 4,4'-MDI). Used for thermoplastic elastomers, coatings, and synthetic leather.
    *   **Polymeric MDI (PMDI):** A dark brown mixture of diisocyanates and higher oligomers. Used to manufacture rigid polyurethane foam for construction insulation and refrigerators.
*   **Polyols:** Organic compounds containing multiple hydroxyl (-OH) groups. They react with isocyanates (MDI/TDI) to form polyurethanes.

---

## 2. Processing & Manufacturing Units

*   **Steam Cracker:** A petrochemical facility that cracks saturated hydrocarbons (like naphtha, butane, or ethane) into light olefins (ethylene, propylene) and pyrolysis gasoline (Pygas) through thermal cracking.
*   **Catalytic Reformer (Platformer):** A refinery unit that converts heavy naphtha with low octane ratings into high-octane liquid products called reformates, which are rich in aromatic hydrocarbons (Benzene, Toluene, Xylene - BTX).
*   **Nitration Unit:** The reactor section of an aniline plant where benzene is continuously nitrated. This reaction is highly exothermic and requires careful temperature control and sulfuric acid recycling.

*   **Hydrogenation Reactor:** The reactor where nitrobenzene is hydrogenated using hydrogen gas over a metal catalyst (usually copper or noble metals) in either vapor or liquid phase to yield aniline and water.
*   **Phosgenation Unit:** The reaction section of an MDI plant where MDA is dissolved in a solvent (typically chlorobenzene) and reacted with phosgene at elevated temperatures to form crude MDI.
*   **Splitter / Fractionation Column:** Distillation columns used to separate chemical mixtures based on boiling points (e.g., separating pure monomeric MDI from crude polymeric MDI mixtures).

*   **Verbund (Integration):** An industrial concept (pioneered by BASF) where production plants, energy flows, and logistics are highly integrated. The by-product of one plant serves as the starting material for the next, reducing waste and logistics costs.

---

## 3. Logistics & Shipping Terminology

*   **Incoterms:** Standardized international commercial terms defining the responsibilities of buyers and sellers for delivery, risk transfer, and freight costs.
    *   **FOB (Free On Board):** The seller delivers the goods on board the vessel nominated by the buyer. Risk transfers at the ship's rail.
    *   **CIF (Cost, Insurance, and Freight):** The seller pays the cost, insurance, and freight to transport the goods to the destination port, but risk transfers to the buyer once the goods are loaded.
    *   **DAP (Delivered at Place):** The seller delivers the goods to a named place, paying all transport costs, but the buyer handles import customs clearance and duties.

*   **DDP (Delivered Duty Paid):** The seller bears all risks and costs, including import duties and taxes, delivering the goods fully cleared to the buyer's destination.
*   **ISO-Tank Container:** An international standard tank container built to ISO standards, designed for the safe, bulk transport of hazardous and non-hazardous liquid chemicals via road, rail, and sea.
*   **Barge:** Flat-bottomed vessels used for inland waterway transport (e.g., transporting benzene along the Rhine River in Europe or the Mississippi River in the US). Highly cost-effective but sensitive to low-water levels.

*   **Pipeline Delivery:** Direct transfer of gaseous or liquid feedstocks (like hydrogen, natural gas, or benzene) between neighboring facilities in a chemical park. Ensures maximum supply reliability.
*   **Tank Farm:** An area of a chemical plant or logistics terminal containing multiple large storage tanks for raw materials, intermediates, and finished products.

---

## 4. Economic & Market Analysis Terms

*   **Spread (Margin Spread):** The price difference between a product and its raw materials (e.g., the *Benzene-Aniline Spread* represents Aniline Price minus Benzene Cost). It indicates the processing margin of the plant.
*   **Cash Cost:** The operational cash outlay required to produce a unit of chemical, including raw materials, energy, utilities, labor, and maintenance, but excluding depreciation and capital costs.
*   **Margin Squeeze:** A situation where raw material costs rise rapidly, but a producer cannot pass those costs to customers immediately (due to lag or poor demand), causing profit margins to shrink.

*   **Force Majeure:** A contract clause that frees both parties from liability or obligation when an extraordinary event beyond their control (e.g., natural disasters, major plant fires, raw material shortages) prevents them from fulfilling their obligations.

THE WOODLANDS, Texas – Huntsman Corporation (NYSE: HUN) today reported fourth quarter 2025 results 
with revenues of $1,355 million, net loss attributable to Huntsman of $96 million, adjusted net loss attributable 
to Huntsman of $63 million and adjusted EBITDA of $35 million.   
 
Peter R. Huntsman, Chairman, President, and CEO, commented: 
 
“During 2025, there was an exceptional amount of work accomplished by the Company in restructuring our 
business and generating cash despite the depressed level of earnings. We generated close to $300 million of 
cash flow from operations in 2025 and our 45% full year free cash flow conversion reflects timely, definitive 
decisions as we recognized the challenging market landscape early in the year. We remain confident that the

decisions as we recognized the challenging market landscape early in the year. We remain confident that the 
economic cycle for chemicals will eventually improve in our core markets, though we recognize that meaningful 
changes may not occur in the immediate term. We are committed to maintaining a disciplined approach, 
prioritizing cash management, the balance sheet and controlling our fixed costs to ensure the Company is well-
positioned when our markets improve."

Footnotes_______________________________________________________________________________ 
 
(1) We use adjusted EBITDA to measure the operating performance of our business and for planning and evaluating the performance 
of our business segments.  We provide adjusted net income (loss) because we feel it provides meaningful insight for the 
investment community into the performance of our business.  We believe that net income (loss) is the performance measure

calculated and presented in accordance with generally accepted accounting principles in the U.S. (“GAAP”) that is most directly 
comparable to adjusted EBITDA and adjusted net income (loss).  Additional information with respect to our use of each of these 
financial measures follows: 
 
Adjusted EBITDA, adjusted net income (loss) and adjusted diluted income (loss) per share, as used herein, are not necessarily 
comparable to other similarly titled measures of other companies.  
 
Adjusted EBITDA is computed by eliminating the following from net income (loss):  (a) net income attributable to noncontrolling 
interests; (b) interest expense, net; (c) income taxes; (d) depreciation and amortization; (e) amortization of pension and

postretirement actuarial losses; (f) restructuring, impairment and plant closing and transition costs; and further adjusted for certain 
other items set forth in the reconciliation of net income (loss) to adjusted EBITDA in Table 4 above.   
 
Adjusted net income (loss) and adjusted diluted income (loss) per share are computed by eliminating the after tax impact of the 
following items from net income (loss): (a) net income attributable to noncontrolling interests; (b) amortization of pension and 
postretirement actuarial losses; (c) restructuring, impairment and plant closing and transition costs; and further adjusted for certain 
other items set forth in the reconciliation of net income (loss) to adjusted net income (loss) in Table 4 above.  The income tax

impacts, if any, of each adjusting item represent a ratable allocation of the total difference between the unadjusted tax expense and 
the total adjusted tax expense, computed without consideration of any adjusting items using a with and without approach. 
 
We may disclose forward-looking adjusted EBITDA because we cannot adequately forecast certain items and events that may or 
may not impact us in the near future, such as business acquisition and integration expenses and purchase accounting inventory 
adjustments, net, certain legal and other settlements and related expenses, gains on sale of businesses/assets and certain tax only 
items, including tax law changes not yet enacted. Each of such adjustment has not yet occurred, is out of our control and/or cannot

be reasonably predicted. In our view, our forward-looking adjusted EBITDA represents the forecast net income on our underlying 
business operations but does not reflect any adjustments related to the items noted above that may occur and can cause our 
adjusted EBITDA to differ.  
 
(2) We believe free cash flow is an important indicator of our liquidity as it measures the amount of cash we generate. Management 
internally uses free cash flow measure to: (a) evaluate our liquidity, (b) evaluate strategic investments, (c) plan stock buyback and 
dividend levels and (d) evaluate our ability to incur and service debt. Free cash flow is defined as net cash provided by (used in)

operating activities less capital expenditures. Free cash flow is not a defined term under U.S. GAAP, and it should not be inferred 
that the entire free cash flow amount is available for discretionary expenditures. 
 
(3) We believe the adjusted effective tax rate provides improved comparability between periods through the exclusion of certain items 
that management believes are not indicative of the businesses’ operational profitability and that may obscure underlying business 
results and trends. In our view, effective tax rate is the performance measure calculated and presented in accordance with U.S. 
GAAP that is most directly comparable to adjusted effective tax rate. The reconciliation of historical adjusted effective tax rate and

effective tax rate is set forth in Table 4 above. Please see the reconciliation of our net income to adjusted net income in Table 4 for 
details regarding the tax impacts of our non-GAAP adjustments. 
 
(4) Net debt is a measure we use to monitor how much debt we have after taking into account our total cash. We use it as an indicator 
of our overall financial position, and calculate it by taking our total debt, including the current portion, and subtracting total cash.

# Industry Reference Guide: Pricing & Contract Methodologies

This guide documents industry-standard contract pricing structures, price index-linkage mechanisms, and surcharge policies in the petrochemical and specialty chemicals sectors. 

It serves as reference material for the RAG database, allowing the platform to reason over pricing terms, contract formulations, and margin squeeze events.

---

## 1. Market Price Benchmarks (ICIS vs. Spot)

In industrial chemical markets, transactions are divided between long-term supply contracts and the spot market:

In industrial chemical markets, transactions are divided between long-term supply contracts and the spot market:

### 1.1 Spot Pricing
*   **Definition:** The price for immediate, short-term transactions (typically delivered within 2–4 weeks).
*   **Characteristics:** Highly volatile, dictated by real-time supply/demand, logistics bottlenecks, plant shutdowns, and regional inventory levels.
*   **Source Data:** Compiled weekly by Independent Price Reporting Agencies (PRAs) like **ICIS**, **Argus Media**, and **S&P Global Platts** through trader interviews and transaction auditing.

### 1.2 Contract Pricing
*   **Definition:** Long-term agreements (usually 1–3 years) ensuring volume security for the buyer and volume baselines for the seller.
*   **Characteristics:** Prices adjust periodically (monthly or quarterly) rather than being fixed. 
*   **Contract Price Benchmarks:** Settled monthly through bilateral agreements between major producers and buyers, then validated and published by PRAs. These published contract prices (e.g., *ICIS Europe Benzene Monthly Contract Price*) are used to settle downstream formulas.

---

## 2. Formula-Based Contract Structures

To share volatility risk, modern chemical supply contracts utilize formula-based pricing linked to upstream feedstock indices.

### 2.1 The Anatomy of a Feedstock-Linked Formula
A standard contract formula dynamically calculates the monthly product price ($P_{\text{Contract}}$) based on the preceding month's average feedstock index ($P_{\text{Feedstock\_Index}}$):

$$P_{\text{Contract}} = \left( \text{Feedstock Index} \times \text{Consumption Factor} \right) + \text{Conversion Premium} \pm \text{Freight}$$

Where:
*   **Feedstock Index:** The monthly published average (e.g., ICIS Benzene Contract Price).
*   **Consumption Factor:** The stoichiometric coefficient representing raw material efficiency (e.g., $0.76$ for Aniline-to-MDI).
*   **Conversion Premium (Add-on):** A fixed premium ($/ton) covering the seller's operating costs, depreciation, and target margin.
*   **Freight/Logistics:** Fixed or variable adjustments covering transportation to the buyer's site (typically under Incoterms like DAP or DDP).

### 2.2 Adjustment Cadence & Timing Lag
*   **Retroactive Settlement:** Some contracts adjust retroactively at the end of the month based on the average index of that same month.
*   **Forward-Looking Settlement (T-1):** Most contracts adjust on the 1st of the month based on the average index of the *previous* month (T-1). This creates a structural **30-day timing lag** between feedstock price changes and finished contract price changes.

---

## 3. Surcharge & Pass-Through Mechanisms

Surcharges are temporary, variable price adjustments implemented by chemical producers to recover extraordinary, volatile input costs that fall outside the standard feedstock formula:

```mermaid
graph TD
    Trigger[Cost Event: e.g., NatGas Spike > 10%] -->|Check| Contract[Contract Surcharge Clause]
    Contract -->|Calculate| Formula[Surcharge = Delta Cost x Utility Factor]
    Formula -->|Apply| Invoice[Base Price + Temporary Surcharge]
```

### 3.1 Energy Surcharges (Gas & Power)
Because chemical plants are highly energy-intensive (requiring high-pressure steam for crackers and reactors), sudden spikes in regional energy markets can severely compress margins.
*   **Energy Surcharge Indexing:** Pegged to regional hub indices (e.g., **Henry Hub** in North America, **Title Transfer Facility (TTF)** in Europe).
*   **Trigger Clause Example:** *"If TTF Natural Gas average price exceeds €50/MWh, a surcharge of €15/ton of product will be applied for every €10/MWh increment above the threshold."*

### 3.2 Logistics & Fuel Surcharges
Added to cover volatile freight costs.
*   **Diesel Fuel Adjusters (DFA):** Surcharges adjusted weekly based on government-published retail diesel prices (e.g., EIA Weekly Retail On-Highway Diesel prices).
*   **Emergency Freight Surcharges:** Temporary fees added during exceptional events (e.g., Rhine River low-water constraints restricting barge loading, ocean container shipping congestion).

### 3.3 Force Majeure & Allocations
During severe supply constraints (e.g., plant fires, raw material force majeure), suppliers may implement **allocations** (restricting supply to a percentage of historical volume) and suspend standard contract pricing, shifting buyers to spot-plus pricing or applying allocation surcharges to cover alternative sourcing costs.

---

## 4. Downstream Customer Pricing Strategy (Huntsman Specific)

Huntsman's Performance Products and Polyurethanes divisions operate on a dual pricing strategy:

1.  **Commodity/Intermediates (Formula-Linked):** Upstream products like maleic anhydride or base polyols are priced using tight **feedstock-plus formulas** linked to benzene/butane.
2.  **Specialty Formulations (Value-in-Use):** Highly engineered downstream polyurethane systems (custom foam packages, lightweight automotive resins) are priced based on the **economic value created for the customer** (e.g., weight savings, energy efficiency improvement), rather than raw material costs.
3.  **Surcharge Activation Policy:** When WTI spikes above $90/bbl, Huntsman's commercial team activates surcharge clauses on value-in-use products to prevent margin compression during the 2–4 week lag before formula contracts reset.
